topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered as I pulled the heavy box onto the worn welcome mat. My hand, usually steady, trembled slightly as I reached for the topmost envelope. It was a thick, cream-colored card, completely blank save for a single, elegantly scripted line at the bottom: "Happy Birthday." My breath hitched in my throat. I stared at the paper, the ink seeming to vibrate under the harsh kitchen light. Who would send this? It wasn't addressed, no return address, just the simple, declarative wish. I let the card slip from my fingers, and it fluttered down onto the linoleum floor like a fallen feather. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out the usual sounds of the morning. I stood frozen, unable to move toward the trash can, my gaze locked on the unsolicited message.

[story 2]

I picked up the card with two fingers, the texture of the cardstock surprisingly smooth against my skin. It was an anomaly in the monotony of the junk mail—no stamps, no sender details, just the words. A cold prickle traced its way down my spine, making the hairs on my arms stand up. I turned it over, searching for any hidden clue, any watermark that might betray an origin. Nothing. Just the message, stark and uninvited. I dropped it back into the mailbox, the sound of the paper hitting the metal reverberating loudly in the quiet hallway. I paced three steps back and forth, my jaw tight, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the counter. Every shadow in the room seemed to deepen, and I couldn't seem to settle my gaze on anything but the spot where the card had rested.

[story 3]

The envelope felt foreign in my hand, heavier than it looked. It was an unsigned birthday greeting, resting among the bills and circulars. As I unfolded it, the silence of the apartment became oppressive. My eyes scanned the paper, searching for any sign of a signature, any familiar handwriting, anything to anchor the object to reality. There was nothing. Only the simple inscription, "Happy Birthday." A strange tightness constricted my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I squeezed my eyes shut for a moment, trying to force the image away, but the words seemed to cling to my vision. When I finally opened my eyes, I felt a sudden, intense need to check the door, to ensure absolute solitude, as if the sender might materialize in the hallway.
